DocuStream · Case Study
Tetiana Dovgalenko - UX/UI Designer

Case Study · Legal-tech SaaS

Designing clarity into a high-stakes filing system

An end-to-end platform for managing UCC filings - a legally regulated process where a single error can have material consequences. Designed to carry complexity without dumping it on the user.

2025 - 2026 UX/UI Designer Enterprise SaaS Legal & Financial domain NDA - screens adapted for portfolio
100+
Legal document types and forms
7+
Distinct filing statuses across the lifecycle
6
User roles for filers, assignees & fulfillment teams

These screens are a narrow slice of a much larger system. DocuStream manages the full lifecycle of UCC filings across US jurisdictions. What is shown here covers the core user workflows: the analytics dashboard, the filings list, the multi-step filing wizard, and the document detail view with its full range of states and post-submission actions.

The problem

A high-stakes process built for specialists - without tools to match

UCC filings sit at the intersection of legal precision and operational scale. A single error - a wrong name format, an omitted county field, a missed lapse deadline - can have material consequences for a secured party. The platform needed to handle hundreds of filings across multiple jurisdictions and workflows, while making the process navigable for users who don't carry all that legal knowledge in their heads.

The challenge was not just information architecture. It was trust: the interface had to communicate enough context for users to act correctly, surface edge cases without overwhelming the main flow, and give teams visibility across a portfolio of filings that could span months and dozens of statuses.

Approach

Complexity surfaced precisely - never hidden, never dumped at once

Working within a cross-functional team alongside developers and domain experts, I designed patterns that could carry legal complexity without collapsing into noise. The filing wizard breaks a multi-jurisdictional document into discrete, manageable steps - each scoped to one concern. The instructions panel opens contextually within the step, so legal guidance is available exactly when needed without blocking the form.

Status management was a particular design challenge. With seven distinct states, every list row, every detail view, every action menu had to communicate where a filing stood and what was possible next. The system also had to handle corner cases: lapse deadlines, paper file requests, multi-page addenda, jurisdiction-specific field requirements, and post-submission actions like Clone, File UCC3, and File UCC5.

Analytics - Dashboard · Screen 01

Dashboard - portfolio overview

The dashboard presents four independent widgets: filings by status (donut chart with enumerated legend), monthly submission volume (dual-series bar chart with trend line), workload distribution by assignee, and lapse tracking. Each widget has its own controls and date range selectors. The layout gives teams the full portfolio picture without opening a single filing.

TaskGive users an immediate read on the health and activity of their entire filing portfolio
BenefitTeams can spot bottlenecks, track monthly submission volume, and identify owners with outstanding work - all from one screen
Donut chartDual-series bar + trend lineWorkload distributionLapse trackingPer-widget controls

Filings list - My filings · Screen 02

My filings - status-driven list view

The filings table shows debtor, filing type, jurisdiction, status, status date, folder, and assignee. Status is colour-coded across seven states. Tab filtering narrows the list by role (All, My filings, Unassigned). Row-level actions expose common operations inline. Pagination and per-page controls handle large portfolios without performance degradation.

TaskLet users scan, filter, and act on hundreds of filings across every stage of the lifecycle
BenefitStatus badges make the state of each filing immediately readable; folder and project columns enable quick portfolio segmentation
Colour-coded status badgesTab filteringFolder & project columnsPaginationRow-level actions

New filing wizard · Screen 03

New filing - step 1: Debtors

The five-step stepper (Debtors → Secured Parties → Collateral → Other → County fields) maps the legal structure of a UCC filing onto a linear, reviewable flow. Each step is scoped to one concern. A persistent metadata sidebar shows filing type and jurisdiction throughout. Address book integration allows reuse of previously entered parties. Partial progress can be saved as draft.

TaskGuide users through creating a legally valid filing across five structured steps
BenefitThe wizard scopes each decision to its own step, reducing cognitive load and the risk of incomplete or invalid submissions
5-step stepperConditional field logicPersistent metadata sidebarAddress bookSave as draft

New filing wizard · Screen 04

New filing - contextual instructions panel

The instructions panel slides in from the right as an overlay alongside the form - not instead of it. It provides field-level legal guidance specific to the current step: what a debtor name must contain, how addresses should be formatted, what distinguishes individual from organisation filers. The panel is closeable and does not interrupt form state.

TaskSurface legal field-by-field guidance without pulling users out of the filing flow
BenefitUsers get the exact guidance they need at the moment they need it, reducing errors and external lookups
Contextual side panelIn-product legal guidanceNon-blocking overlayField-level instructions

Filing detail view · Screen 05

Filing detail - document view with tab navigation

The filing detail view presents the document in its legal form - field labels match the official UCC form layout. Tab navigation handles multi-page documents and addenda. A persistent metadata sidebar shows filing type, status, jurisdiction, and key dates. The status badge in the header communicates filing state at a glance.

TaskPresent the full content of a submitted filing across multiple document pages in its legal form
BenefitUsers can review and verify the complete filing record without leaving the platform
Tab navigation for multi-page documentsOfficial field label mappingPersistent metadata sidebarStatus badge in header

Filing detail view · Screen 06

Filing detail - Actions dropdown

The Actions dropdown exposes five operations: Clone, Edit, File UCC3, File UCC5, and Add to folder. Each action is contextually appropriate to the filing's current status. Destructive or irreversible operations are visually distinguished. The dropdown pattern keeps the document view clean while making all operations discoverable.

TaskExpose post-submission actions - continuation, amendment, termination - without cluttering the document view
BenefitSpecialist UCC actions (UCC3, UCC5) are accessible from context but don't compete with the reading experience
Actions dropdownClone & EditFile UCC3 / UCC5Contextual follow-on actions

Filing detail view · Screen 07

Filing detail - Complete state with warning banner

When a filing reaches Complete status, the document view shows the official stamped form. A warning banner communicates the jurisdiction-specific caveat: the system-generated document is a record, not the authoritative filed copy. The sidebar fields update to reflect post-completion metadata. The layout adapts to the new state without structural changes.

TaskCommunicate a completed filing's state accurately, including jurisdiction-specific caveats about what the system-generated document represents
BenefitUsers understand exactly what the system-generated document represents - and what it does not replace
Warning bannerJurisdiction caveatDynamic sidebar fieldsStatus-driven layout changes

Filing detail view · Screen 08

Filing detail - Details panel

The Details panel slides in from the right when the info button is activated, overlaying the document view. It surfaces the official file number assigned by the jurisdiction, the confirmed filing date, and a direct link to the filed copy. The overlay pattern is consistent with the in-wizard instructions panel, reducing learning cost across very different contexts.

TaskSurface official filing metadata - file number, filing date, jurisdiction, and direct link to the filed copy
BenefitOperations teams get the jurisdiction-confirmed record details and a direct link to the official document in one place
Details side panelOfficial file numberJurisdiction confirmationFiled copy linkConsistent overlay pattern

Filing detail view · Screen 09

Evidence cover letter - modal

When a filing reaches Acknowledged status, the system generates a cover letter for the client. The modal presents this letter in full, including the acknowledgment reference, the jurisdiction-specific caveat language, and a link to the filed document. The modal pattern isolates the letter from the main filing view without navigating away.

TaskPresent the system-generated client communication that accompanies an acknowledged filing
BenefitUsers can review the exact letter sent to the client - including the acknowledgment number and any jurisdiction caveats
Modal overlaySystem-generated letterAcknowledged status flowDocument link in modalJurisdiction caveat

Results

What the system makes possible

Filing teams can now move through a UCC workflow from creation to completion within a single platform - without consulting external documentation, switching between tools, or losing track of where a filing stands. Legal guidance is embedded at the point of need; post-submission actions are contextual and correctly scoped; document history and metadata are always one click away.

Learnings

What this project reinforced

Domain knowledge is a design material

Understanding the difference between a UCC3 and a UCC5, or what makes a debtor name legally insufficient, directly shapes every label, every warning, every action in the interface. The closer the designer gets to the domain, the more precise the decisions become.

Edge cases are the product

In a regulated workflow, the exceptions are not exceptions - they are the product. Warning banners, jurisdiction caveats, conditional fields, lapse tracking: every one required the same attention as the main flow. Treating corner cases as afterthoughts produces interfaces that fail the people who need them most.

Consistent patterns earn trust

Using the same right-side overlay pattern for both in-wizard instructions and post-submission detail panels reduced cognitive overhead across very different contexts. When a system behaves predictably, users extend it more trust - which matters especially in high-stakes, legally consequential workflows.
